author: Watson T;McCutchen R
title: #BlackLivesMatter:A call for transformative leadership
argument_summary: The article presents a case study of a high school principal confronted with the #BlackLivesMatter movement, arguing that effective educational leadership in racially diverse urban contexts requires a shift from technical, results-oriented management to transformative leadership practices that explicitly center race, social justice, and community engagement.
relevant_quote: the #BlackLivesMatter movement, “a call to action and a response to the virulent anti-Black racism that permeates our society

author: Arif A;Stewart L;Starbird K
title: Acting the Part:Examining Information Operations Within #BlackLivesMatter Discourse
argument_summary: The article shows that information operations infiltrated #BlackLivesMatter discourse through coordinated fake accounts that mimicked users, amplified polarization, and manipulated online narratives.
relevant_quote: accounts associated with the Russian Internet Research Agency (RU-IRA) participated in an online discourse about the #BlackLivesMatter movement, imitated ordinary users, and fostered antagonism and undermined trust in information intermediaries

author: Garza A
title: A herstory of the #BlackLivesMatter movement,Seattle Public Schools (Lesson Plan)
argument_summary: The text presents a narrative account of the origins and development of #BlackLivesMatter, arguing that the hashtag functions as a political and ideological intervention against systemic anti-Black racism, evolving from a digital call to action into a broader social movement that affirms Black lives, resists erasure, and articulates struggles across multiple axes of oppression.
temporal_markers: Michael Brown killing and Ferguson protests (2014)
Mainstream media and corporate appropriation of the hashtag (mid-2010s)
Adaptations such as #AllLivesMatter and similar slogans (mid-2010s)
Mass incarceration of Black populations in the U.S. (structural)
relevant_quote: Black Lives Matter is an ideological and political intervention in a world where Black lives are systematically and intentionally targeted for demise.

author: Bassett MT
title: #BlackLivesMatter — A Challenge to the Medical and Public Health Communities
argument_summary: The article argues that the #BlackLivesMatter movement exposes how systemic racism shapes health inequalities, calling on medical and public health professionals to move beyond clinical care and actively address structural racism through research, institutional reform, and public advocacy.
temporal_markers: Eric Garner death and grand jury decision (2014)
#BlackLivesMatter movement expansion in the United States (2014–2015)
White coat die-ins by medical students (2014–2015)
Contemporary public debates on racism and policing (mid-2010s)
Historical legacy of medical experimentation on Black Americans (long duration)
World Health Organization definition of health (1948)
Persistent racial health disparities in the United States (structural)
Ongoing institutional and interpersonal racism in healthcare (structural)
relevant_quote: Should health professionals be accountable not only for caring for individual black patients but also for fighting the racism — both institutional and interpersonal — that contributes to poor health in the first place?

author: Chen JN
title: #BlackLivesMatter and the State of Asian/America
argument_summary: The article argues that #BlackLivesMatter functions as a critical framework for analyzing systemic anti-Black racism and its historical continuities, while also challenging Asian American Studies to confront U.S. state violence and imperialism, fostering solidarities that recognize how racial formations and inequalities are structured through interconnected histories of colonialism, slavery, and migration.
temporal_markers: Michael Brown killing and Ferguson protests (2014)
Civil Rights and Black Power movements (1950s–1960s)
Post–civil rights restructuring of the U.S. state (mid-1960s onward)
Slavery and antebellum period in the United States (long duration)
Post-slavery racial criminalization and convict leasing (late 19th–early 20th century)
Mass incarceration and policing regimes (late 20th–21st century)
1965 Immigration Act and U.S. immigration restructuring (1965)
Cold War U.S. imperial expansion (20th century)
Contemporary Black liberation movements and transnational solidarities (21st century)
relevant_quote: #BLM’s unapologetic affirming of the mattering of black lives is therefore no less than a mobilization to overturn the antiblackness that founds the modern U.S. nation-state
